thresholdSupport.notes: American Whitewater has the exact Ponca-to-Kyle's Landing Class II reach tied to the Ponca gauge and showed 206 cfs as medium runnable during review; its trip evidence describes 130 cfs as close to the minimum for a loaded tandem canoe. NPS says levels above 1,600 cfs at Ponca are unsafe. The implemented model is minimum-only at 130 cfs, with the NPS high value in route caveats rather than a full two-sided score.

gauge.notes: USGS Buffalo River at Pruitt is the downstream take-out corridor gauge for the route. The official legacy page exposed recent values during review: 442 cfs and 4.85 ft at 2026-05-31 16:30 CDT.

thresholdSupport.notes: The NPS-linked Buffalo National River Float Guide publishes Pruitt bands of Very Low below 100 cfs, Low from 100-200 cfs, Moderate from 200-1,000 cfs, High from 1,000-2,000 cfs, and Flood above 2,000 cfs. NPS paddling guidance separately says levels above 2,000 cfs at Pruitt/Hwy. 7 are unsafe. The app uses only a conservative 100 cfs minimum floor and does not claim an ideal range.

endpoints.coordinateSource: NPS Getting Around lists Kyle's Landing and Pruitt launch coordinate anchors. NPS access/mileage chart confirms the route pair and intermediate Erbie/Ozark access sequence.

candidates.notes: Added 2026-06-01 as a guarded routeType whitewater Buffalo National River gap-fill between the live Ponca-to-Kyle's and Pruitt-to-Hasty routes. NPS supports the access chain and 13.2-mile route shape; AW supports the exact Class I-II Kyle's-to-Pruitt reach with Erbie and Ozark intermediate access; USGS 07055680 remains the product-supported live gauge; and NPS Float Guide Pruitt bands support minimum-only 100 cfs scoring. Caveats cover rain dependence, long mileage, swift water, strainers/sweepers/fresh wood, rough Kyle's Landing shuttle road, limited cell service, low-water dragging, high/rising water, cold water, and remote rescue exposure. candidates.notes: Added 2026-05-31 as a guarded routeType whitewater Buffalo National River follow-on. Access and route evidence are strong: NPS describes the 7-mile / 3-5 hour Pruitt-to-Hasty trip, says the Little Buffalo adds velocity, identifies Hasty on river right across from Chimney Rock Bluff, and says the route is not recommended for beginners. candidates.retryCondition: Already implemented as mulberry-river-redding-turner-bend. Revisit only if Forest Service or Turner Bend access terms change, AW/USGS threshold calibration changes, endpoint coordinates improve, or a rights-clean route photo can be ingested.

gauge.notes: The route uses USGS 07252000 Mulberry River near Mulberry, AR. It is downstream of the short Redding-to-Turner-Bend reach, but American Whitewater explicitly ties this exact reach to the USGS 07252000 stage range, and official USGS legacy current conditions exposed same-day May 31, 2026 discharge and gage-height values during review.

thresholdSupport.notes: American Whitewater lists USGS 07252000 at 1.55-6.00 ft for the exact Redding Campground-to-Highway-23 / Turner Bend Class I-III reach, describing the envelope as barely runnable to high runnable. Paddle Today uses that exact AW stage envelope as conservative two-sided scoring and does not import Turner Bend staff-gauge sub-bands as the product gauge.

endpoints.coordinateSource: Forest Service Redding Recreation Area official coordinates for the put-in. Turner Bend take-out uses the Highway 23 / Mulberry River bridge corridor coordinate from the Commons NRHP bridge file, backed by Turner Bend access pages documenting the landing at the Highway 23 / Mulberry River junction.

candidates.notes: Implemented 2026-06-01 UTC as a guarded Arkansas whitewater route. The previous blocker was resolved by AW reach-specific USGS stage support. Remaining caveats are high: private-fee Turner Bend access, staff-gauge versus USGS-gauge nuance, rain dependence, fast rises, Class I-III rapids, strainers/fresh wood, rural shuttle, limited cell service, and no casual-float positioning.

thresholdSupport.notes: AW says 200-500 cfs continuous release is enough to get down the route with some scraping and normal generation is 3,500-4,000 cfs. Entergy independently says flows below about 3,200 cfs may be too low for an enjoyable tube float, 3,500-4,000 cfs is a good recreational level, and flows above 4,000 cfs are not recommended for floating. PaddleToday uses 200 cfs as a scrape-through floor, 3,500-4,000 cfs as the ideal release window, and 4,000 cfs as the conservative upper cutoff.

The put-in coordinate is the USGS/AW Remmel Dam gauge/access-area anchor adjacent to Entergy's below-dam launch instructions. The take-out coordinate is the Mapcarta/OpenStreetMap anchor for Ouachita River Whitewater Park at 904 Riverview Drive. Treat both as practical access-area anchors and follow posted signs on arrival.

candidates.route: Indianapolis White River Canoe Trail family
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-22 threshold refresh plus exact-subreach tightening. The public route definition is cleaner now than the older citywide family. Current Indy Parks pages still confirm the White River canoe-route access network at Broad Ripple Park, the Indianapolis Arts Center, and Riverside Regional Park, and the Art's Center Canoe Launch page still gives a formal public launch at 900 E 67th St. Frank's current White River trip page now makes the two downstream shapes explicit: Broad Ripple to Riverside is a 7-mile trip and Rocky Ripple to Riverside is a 4-mile trip. candidates.route: Potter's Bridge Park / Forest Park / Noblesville family
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-21 threshold refresh plus family low-water-context recheck. This Noblesville family still has one of the cleaner Indiana access packages, but it is not implementation-ready. Official Hamilton County and Noblesville pages still support Potter's Bridge Park canoe launch and the Forest Park White River Boat Ramp as real public endpoints, and Discover White River's current paddling page explicitly lists Potter's Bridge Park and Forest Park among the popular put-in and takeout points for the Noblesville section. White River Canoe Company still treats Potter's Bridge as the normal longer float and says late-season low water shifts users to the shorter Forest Park trip. IndianaOutfitters still preserves a nearby Hoosier Canoe Club report of a nice/swift Perkinsville-to-Noblesville trip at 430 cfs on gauge 03349000, and Hoosier Canoe Club's April 26 2026 Potter's Bridge-to-116th Street newsletter report says the family still had a line through low water at about 3.65 ft on the 146th Street gauge. That is better family-level low-water context than the earlier operator fallback alone, but it still does not create a source-backed low-water floor or exact route-specific scoring model for Potter's Bridge-to-Forest Park on gauge 03349000. Keep the lead blocked on missing exact-route minimums and unresolved low-head-dam/private-bank handling around Clare and downstream Noblesville.

thresholdSupport.notes: AW provides direct reach/gauge linkage and runnable-state context. Recent AW trip reports suggest 205 cfs is low/scrapy and around 250 cfs is better for packrafts; the route page warns around 600-700 cfs may make the Croyden bridge impassable and notes higher-flow holes at 1400+ cfs. Implementation remains blocked by endpoint authority rather than current gauge availability. Use conservative whitewater scoring; do not overclaim a polished broad-audience ideal range without implementation review.

endpoints.putIn: Henefer-Echo Angler Access WMA / western Henefer Exit 112 main dirt parking access
endpoints.takeOut: Taggarts river-right take-out below Taggarts Falls / I-84 Exit 108 area
endpoints.coordinateSource: Coordinates remain unresolved. Current Utah DWR data now does more than expose a broad WMA reference: the live Recreation Access property table exposes WEBER RIVER ACCESSHENEFER as active property ID {5B4F2C09-3869-4556-87A1-629A3744C6B5}, and a fresh token-backed direct query of the official WMA polygon layer now returns the actual compact WEBER RIVER ACCESS-HENEFER access parcel in WGS84 rather than only a generic parcel reference. The official polygon brackets the access corridor itself, with vertices running roughly from -111.51112001441005, 41.038156962246845 up to -111.52200680527869, 41.044169733358224, and the same record still says the site provides just over a half-mile of river access with two large parking lots near Henefer exit 112. That materially strengthens upstream public-use legitimacy, but the paired official WMA point layer still returns zero Henefer point features and no exact launch/parking coordinate, so the official story remains polygon-only rather than route-ready. Downstream remains weaker even after the new county GIS pass: Morgan County's current official GIS Data web experience and backing web map expose Taggart-area road centerlines and nearby private residential parcels, but no named county river-access feature, public parking parcel, or route-ready take-out coordinate below Taggarts Falls. Utah WRI still describes the Taggarts take-out area as county-owned old highway off the Taggart Exit rather than an officially established boat ramp. Nearby Henefer DWR fishing-access parcels such as BStevens explicitly say they are not authorized public access or exit points for recreational tubing or rafting, so they cannot be substituted as paddling endpoints. Supplemental Outbound coordinates and RiverBrain directions remain below the authority needed to ship this private-bank-sensitive route.

candidates.notes: Strong route-shape and threshold lead, and the upstream public-access story is now materially stronger because current Utah DWR data now supports WEBER RIVER ACCESS-HENEFER in four aligned ways: the live property table row, the same property ID, the official descriptive text about two large parking lots, and now the actual compact WMA polygon geometry that brackets the access corridor near exit 112. The downstream story is now split more clearly between positive and negative official signals: Morgan County public-notice materials and Utah Commerce Advisory Opinion 286 still support the county-asset / right-of-way theory, but the current official Morgan County GIS package only surfaces Taggart-area road features and nearby private residential parcels rather than a named public river-access or parking feature. Direct USGS Water Services is also working again on the route gauge, so the remaining blocker is still endpoint authority only: neither endpoint has a source-backed implementation coordinate, the official DWR point layer still does not pin the Henefer launch, Taggarts still lacks an official route-ready take-out point or current operating parking rules, and nearby Henefer fishing-access parcels are now explicitly shown as invalid tubing/rafting substitutes. Treat as Class II(III) whitewater/swiftwater if later implemented, with rock garden, Taggarts Falls, Croyden bridge/portage, private-bank, legal-access, parking-fee, tubing-crowd, cold-water, and high-flow bridge/hole caveats.

gauge.notes: Implemented with USGS 09234500 Green River near Greendale, UT as the direct tailwater gauge immediately below Flaming Gorge Dam. thresholdSupport.notes: Implemented as minimum-only at 1,000 cfs on USGS 09234500. American Whitewater ties the broader Flaming Gorge-to-Lodore reach to the Greendale gauge and showed the reach runnable at 1,000 cfs; Recreation.gov/Ashley support the exact 7-mile Section A route as Class I-II. Apple River looked like a possible northwest Illinois whitewater/access lead because American Whitewater's Illinois gauge list includes USGS 05419000 Apple River near Hanover with current-looking flow/stage context, and IDNR confirms Apple River Canyon State Park is public state-park land along the river. The route was not added because no normal public put-in/take-out day-trip pair, official canoe/kayak access page, or manager/water-trail route shape surfaced. IDNR's Apple River Canyon page emphasizes hiking, fishing, hunting, camping, and picnicking rather than boating access, and the only strong current evidence found was gauge-level rather than route-level. Do not ship from gauge existence plus park adjacency in Illinois.

candidates.route: Blue Star Memorial Woods to Linne Woods / suburban North Branch family
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-22 stale-blocker audit. The threshold gap is still no longer the primary blocker. Miles Paddled's 2023 North Branch route report remains the best same-gauge community model on USGS 05536000 with a 75 cfs minimum, roughly 100 cfs median, the Chick Evans check dam washed out around 300 cfs, and low golf-course bridge headroom still passable up to about 350 cfs. A fresh USGS Water Services IV recheck returned 101 cfs and 1.58 ft at 2026-06-22 02:30 CDT, which keeps the family inside that conservative envelope. The reason this lead is no longer close-to-ready is access legitimacy and exact route selection: same-day Forest Preserves of Cook County rechecks still make Linne Woods the only named North Branch canoe landing, the current Blue Star page still advertises only preserve/trail amenities, and the repeated June 20-22 source loop did not surface a second manager-backed ordinary launch or landing to pair with Linne. Park this lead until a stronger manager/source package publishes a normal public Blue Star, Bunker Hill, or other North Branch landing with coordinates or clearly mapped access, then re-attach the urban low-bridge/logjam/CSO caveats to that exact subreach.

candidates.route: Castle Rock State Park to Lowell Park / Dixon family
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-21 exact-route tightening pass. IDNR still explicitly lists Castle Rock State Park as Rock River public canoe/kayak access and says a trip to Lowell Park or Dixon Dam is about 12 miles. Miles Paddled's Rock River I report makes the future implementation shape less vague by recommending the shorter Castle Rock boat landing to Lowell Park trip instead of the full Oregon-to-Dixon paddle, while recording the broader Oregon-to-Dixon run at Como 5,300 cfs and 4.10 ft in 2013 and describing levels as almost always reliable. The direct gauge story remains workable because USGS 05443500 at Como still has a same-river live path. The lead still should not be promoted because the numeric threshold package remains too thin: MoHERP/legacy Rock River Como data still shows only two route-family Good rows around 11,500-11,900 cfs and 5.44-5.55 ft, and Miles Paddled's single observed 5,300 cfs day plus qualitative reliability note do not create a defensible low-water floor, ideal range, or high-water cutoff for the Castle Rock-to-Lowell subreach.

candidates.route: Hazen Bridge to Lake of the Woods Forest Preserve
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-20 upper-Sangamon narrowing pass. Sangamon River Alliance's current site still preserves the exact USRC Hazen Bridge to Lake of the Woods float-trip write-up, including the named route, 5.5-mile distance, and Fisher gauge guidance: 4.5 ft was lower than recommended, 5.5 to 6 ft is better for enjoyment, and the trip should stay below about 10 ft flood stage. That was enough to prove the old Lincoln Heritage family contains one real exact upper-Sangamon subreach. But the stronger current official access signal cuts against promotion: Champaign County Forest Preserve's current 2040 Comprehensive Plan snippets say Hazen Bridge is currently inaccessible to visitors and that the preserve is still exploring boat/canoe/kayak access on the Sangamon, while a 2021 official board-packet snippet says canoe-launch and parking-lot construction was underway near the Izaak Walton Cabin. Until current manager-grade sources confirm the completed launch/take-out arrangement and public paddler use, this narrowed Hazen-to-Lake-of-the-Woods lead is parked as blocked_until_date rather than left in active access churn.
candidates.retryCondition: Retry only if Champaign County Forest Preserve District, Sangamon River Alliance, Upper Sangamon River Conservancy, or another accepted manager-grade source confirms Hazen Bridge is open to visitors for paddler access, confirms the Lake of the Woods / Izaak Walton cabin finish as a normal public take-out, and publishes exact landing coordinates or a clearly designated paddler-access feature.

candidates.route: Lower Cache River Canoe Trail
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-20 product-fit refresh. The public trail story remains strong, but the route looks less like a normal gauge-scored river segment than it did on the seed pass. Miles Paddled's 2024 Lower Cache report corroborates the public Access Road launch, describes the trail as a choose-your-own-adventure 4-mile loop in essentially still swamp water, notes that the paddling area is held up by earthen dams so there is usually enough water to paddle, and mentions only an on-site staff gauge reading of 328 near the bridge without tying it to a product-fetchable USGS rule. candidates.route: Knoch Knolls Park / Naperville river-trip family
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-22 threshold refresh plus public-access tightening. The access story is now materially stronger and the future public pair is cleaner. Current Naperville Park District materials still list Knoch Knolls Park at 336 Knoch Knolls Road as an easy boat/canoe launch, and the district FAQ now says Naperville maintains four designated canoe/kayak launch sites on its DuPage corridor and has jurisdiction over park-property shoreline access to the river. Openlands still frames Knoch Knolls Park to Eaton Preserve as a beginner-to-intermediate trip of about 7 miles, Plainfield Park District now explicitly says Eaton Preserve has an improved canoe/kayak launch site with parking, and current Will County pages confirm Riverview Farmstead Preserve also has a canoe/kayak launch with no launch pass required. That means public put-in/take-out legitimacy is no longer the weak side of this lead. The route still should not be promoted because the numeric guidance remains too weak: IllinoisPaddle's scrape/action/flood stages are still supplemental local notes rather than a defensible route-specific scoring model, and this pass still did not establish one selected product-live gauge with honest low/high thresholds for an exact public endpoint pair.
candidates.retryCondition: Retry only if Naperville, Openlands, Plainfield Park District, Will County, or another strong route-specific source publishes numeric paddling guidance tied to a selected live gauge for Knoch Knolls Park to Eaton Preserve or another selected official launch pair, and if current manager-backed coordinates can then be captured for the chosen endpoints.

candidates.route: Lenon Mill Park Access to Middle Raccoon River Access
candidates.blocker: Official Iowa DNR, Guthrie County, and Dallas County materials support the Middle Raccoon water-trail corridor from Lenon Mill Park Access through P-28 Access / Middle Raccoon River Access, and USGS 05483600 Middle Raccoon River at Panora is a direct live gauge in the route corridor. thresholdSupport.notes: MilesPaddled records exact upper-Sugar trips at 53, 56, and 64 cfs on the Verona gauge, treating 53 cfs as just enough and warning that 2.5-3 ft is shallow while above 4 ft is wild and unruly. Wisconsin River Trips records 64 cfs as a good depth, treats about 100 cfs as a pretty full river, and cautions against high-water paddling because of fast current and downed trees. The app uses this as a guarded two-sided CFS model: 55-100 cfs preferred, below 50 cfs shallow/slow, and 200+ cfs outside casual recommendation.

candidates.route: Cedar Valley Paddlers Trail / George Wyth State Park and Waterloo-Cedar Falls corridor
candidates.blocker: 2026-06-21 queue-quality recheck. This lead no longer belongs in the closest threshold lane. The current Cedar Valley Trails manager pages still frame the Cedar Valley Paddlers' Trail as a 10-mile loop with 8.4 miles of water, 1.6 miles of portage, and six access points split across George Wyth State Park and Hartman Reserve rather than a single trust-first point-to-point Cedar River route. The live-gauge path at USGS 05463050 still works, and prior official Cedar Valley water-trail guidance still provides only a useful high-water ceiling below 7,500 cfs, not a defensible low-water floor or ideal range. Until a selected subreach and a minimum-only or two-sided gauge model exist, this family is better parked as later research than treated as near-ready threshold cleanup.
candidates.retryCondition: Retry only if Iowa DNR, Cedar Falls/Waterloo, Black Hawk County, Cedar Valley Paddlers Trail managers, or another strong route source publishes one selected point-to-point paddling segment with a low-water floor or fuller range tied to USGS 05463050, or if the product adds explicit maximum-only scoring semantics.

This route clears a guarded Wisconsin quality bar with named guide access points, published endpoint coordinates, a direct same-river live USGS gauge, route-specific MilesPaddled water-level evidence, and Wisconsin Trail Guide route/hazard documentation. Confidence remains below the best Wisconsin additions because the threshold model is based on one same-route community observation rather than a full official or community ladder, and official manager access pages are thinner than for DNR landings. No route-gallery asset was added because the image audit still has no clearly reusable representative White River route photo.
2026-06-17 safety audit: removed from shipped route data and rejected. Rejected during route safety audit. The route ends at the Highway 112 dam with weaker official landing/parking authority than the app should accept for a dam-adjacent take-out, and the moving-water/pin-risk profile raises the consequence of a missed or unclear exit.

thresholdSupport.notes: Wisconsin River Trips publishes route-specific Colfax-gauge guidance for this Red Cedar reach: under 400 cfs still navigable, 401-600 cfs good depth / typical fall, 601-800 cfs a bit high and pushy, 801-1200 cfs high and expert-only because of strainers, and 1201+ cfs maybe too high. The app uses 401-600 cfs as the preferred window and 801+ cfs as beyond broad recommendation.

endpoints.coordinateSource: Wisconsin River Trips' Google shuttle map resolves the planned endpoint coordinates. Paddling.com independently lists Tom Prince Memorial Park on the Red Cedar River at 45.002775, -91.729783. Dunn County corroborates Russian Slough as county-owned public Red Cedar corridor land, and Wisconsin DNR's 2024 Red Cedar River survey lists Russian Slough among notable public accesses.

candidates.notes: Implemented 2026-05-25 as a guarded Wisconsin route after the prompted Bark and Sugar routes were already implemented and Black Earth Creek remained blocked. This route clears the quality bar with named public endpoints, endpoint coordinates, a direct same-river USGS gauge, route-specific community level guidance, and explicit strainer/high-water cautions. The main caveat is Russian Slough: Dunn County confirms it as public land but says it has no amenities or developed boat landing and may close during spring walleye spawning, so the app presents it as a rustic take-out that must be scouted. No route-gallery asset was added because no clearly reusable representative route photo was found.

thresholdSupport.notes: Wisconsin River Trips publishes route-family Stratford-gauge guidance for the Big Eau Pleine: under 100 cfs likely too shallow, 101-150 cfs runnable with bumps, 151-200 cfs good, 201-250 cfs also good, 251-300 cfs high but manageable for experienced paddlers, 301-400 cfs high for recreational paddlers but good for whitewater paddlers, 401-500 cfs likely whitewater-only, and 501+ cfs very high / very experienced paddlers only. The app uses 151-250 cfs as the preferred window and 301+ cfs as beyond broad recommendation.

endpoints.coordinateSource: AnyplaceAmerica publishes USGS/topographic-place coordinates for Cherokee Park and March Rapids County Park. Marathon County corroborates Cherokee Park as a public county park on the Big Eau Pleine River, and Wisconsin River Trips corroborates March Rapids Park as the public take-out with paths and pull-off space.

thresholdSupport.notes: The Iowa DNR / Hamilton County water-trail brochure says the Riverside-to-Briggs-Woods stretch is good for all skill levels during normal water conditions and that low water exposes riffles and boulders. Hamilton County's touring sheet adds a useful numeric high-water skill split by saying Boone River conditions at 1,100 cfs or below are good for inexperienced canoeists and 1,500 cfs and above are advanced-only. American Whitewater's Boone Ledge page adds same-gauge feature-specific context at 1,000 to 2,200 cfs below Albright's, but that still leaves the route below the add bar because no defensible low-water floor or preferred band was found for the exact Riverside-to-Briggs reach.

Keep any future Boone work focused on genuinely distinct subreaches rather than reopening this overlapping segment as a separate threshold lead.

candidates.route: Whitewater Drive / Fillmore area to Highway D61
candidates.blocker: Dubuque County confirms public canoe/kayak access context for Whitewater Creek, and Miles Paddled plus Wisconsin River Trips document the canyon route with numeric level guidance, but the only live gauge support found is USGS 05418400 North Fork Maquoketa River near Fulton. That gauge is on the downstream receiving river rather than Whitewater Creek itself, and the published bands are explicitly rough / minimum-oriented. This is useful trip-planning evidence but not strong enough for a new PaddleToday route under the current Iowa quality bar.
candidates.retryCondition: Revisit if USGS, Iowa Flood Center, Dubuque County, Iowa DNR, Miles Paddled, Wisconsin River Trips, or a local paddling manager publishes a Whitewater Creek-specific live gauge or stronger route-specific level bands tied cleanly to a gauge that reflects Whitewater Creek conditions.
sourcesChecked: Dubuque County Paddling page
Miles Paddled Whitewater Creek
Wisconsin River Trips Whitewater Creek
USGS 05418400 North Fork Maquoketa River near Fulton monitoring location
Iowa DNR Fish Iowa Maquoketa River map PDF / Whitewater Creek note
Wikimedia Commons / USGS / Iowa DNR / Dubuque County photo search
aliases: Whitewater Creek - Whitewater Drive to Highway D61
Whitewater Creek - Fillmore to D61
Whitewater Creek - Whitewater Canyon

gauge.notes: Miles Paddled and Wisconsin River Trips both tie the route to USGS 05418400 North Fork Maquoketa River near Fulton, but Whitewater Creek is a tributary that enters the North Fork downstream of the route. The gauge is useful context after rain, yet it is not a direct same-creek gauge.

thresholdSupport.notes: Miles Paddled reports the route at 3.2 ft / 380 cfs and says the minimum recommended level is roughly 3.5 to 4 ft or about 600 cfs on the Fulton gauge. Wisconsin River Trips gives an educated-guess ladder: under 300 cfs may be too low, 301-400 cfs doable but scrape-prone, 501-600 cfs high, and 601+ cfs a stay-off preference. Both are useful, but the proxy-gauge and rough-band caveats keep this below the app route bar.

endpoints.coordinateSource: Miles Paddled publishes GPS coordinates for Whitewater Drive and Highway D61 on the route report. Dubuque County confirms public canoe/kayak access to Whitewater Creek and names D61 Access as a true access, while Wisconsin River Trips corroborates D61 as a popular take-out with parking and a mowed path.

candidates.notes: Deferred 2026-05-25 after the main Iowa candidates were already implemented or intentionally deferred. Whitewater Creek is attractive and likely worth revisiting, especially because Dubuque County confirms access context and both community reports describe a scenic canyon route. The current blocker is gauge quality rather than route interest. No gallery asset was added because the found route imagery was from route-report/blog sources or county context without a clearly reusable exact-route license.

candidates.route: Mines of Spain canoe launch round trip
candidates.blocker: Catfish Creek has a named public carry-down access at Mines of Spain and strong route-report evidence, but the level story depends on Mississippi River at Clayton stage rather than a direct live Catfish Creek gauge. Wisconsin River Trips explicitly notes there is no Catfish Creek gauge and that Mississippi backwater level is what matters near the mouth. That is useful local planning context, but it does not meet the Iowa route quality bar for a direct same-river/route-corridor gauge.
candidates.retryCondition: Retry only if USGS, Iowa DNR, Mines of Spain, Dubuque County, Wisconsin River Trips, Miles Paddled, or another credible source publishes Catfish Creek-specific live level data or a manager-endorsed route condition source strong enough to replace the Mississippi backwater proxy.

gauge.notes: USGS 05389000 Yellow River near Ion, IA is a direct live same-river gauge in the lower Yellow River corridor. Wisconsin River Trips uses this gauge for the Yellow River ladder, and Miles Paddled reports the exact lower route against the Ion gauge.

thresholdSupport.notes: Wisconsin River Trips publishes the Ion-gauge Yellow River ladder: below 50 cfs likely too low, 51-100 cfs very shallow, 101-200 cfs shallow, 201-300 cfs average/good target, 301-600 cfs above average/high, 601-1000 cfs very high and expert-oriented, and 1001+ cfs maybe too high. Miles Paddled separately says the lower route needs enough water, identifies 80 cfs as a minimum caution, and ran it at 130 cfs.

endpoints.coordinateSource: Put-in coordinates reuse the existing Bridge Sixteen Canoe Access public-access coordinate already supported in app research, while Miles Paddled publishes matching Old Sixteen Road route GPS context and WRT directs paddlers to the public launch downstream/river left of Sixteen Bridge. Take-out coordinates come from Miles Paddled route GPS context for the Highway 76 / railroad bridge mouth-area finish, corroborated by NPS South Unit / Yellow River Boardwalk access context and Iowa DNR lower Yellow River State Forest canoe-access mapping.

candidates.notes: Implemented 2026-05-26 as a focused Iowa follow-on after the prompted candidates were already present. gauge.notes: USGS 07071500 Eleven Point River near Bardley, MO is a direct same-river lower-Eleven-Point gauge downstream of the Whitten-to-Riverton reach. The current product can fetch this standard USGS instantaneous-values path, and American Whitewater ties the broader Highway 19-to-Highway 160 reach to the Bardley gauge.

thresholdSupport.notes: Rivers.MOHERP rates the Bardley gauge good beginning at 300 cfs and includes exact Whitten-to-Riverton trip evidence at 644 cfs saying the water was just high enough to navigate nearly every riffle without hitting rocks. The app therefore uses only a conservative 300 cfs low-water floor and does not claim an ideal range or upper cutoff.

endpoints.coordinateSource: Forest Service Whitten River Access and Riverton East River Access pages publish both endpoint coordinates and describe the access facilities. Forest Service also explicitly describes Whitten-to-Riverton as an 8-mile popular day float.

candidates.notes: Implemented 2026-05-26 as the first Missouri V2 route. It clears the conservative bar with official public endpoints, official coordinates and open access pages, a practical 8-mile day-float shape, a direct live USGS gauge, and a guarded community low-water floor. Caveats remain around lack of official paddling bands, no high-water cutoff, cold water, motorized users, Halls Bay channel choice, rapid rises after rain, and private land away from public access or legal stops. No gallery asset was added.

candidates.route: Dexter-Huron / Delhi Metropark day-trip family
candidates.blocker: Fresh 2026-05-29 18:09 America/Chicago recheck preserved the blocker. Metroparks, HRWC / Huron River Water Trail, Skip's, and Outdoor Michigan support a real Dexter-Huron-to-Delhi route with public launches, livery service, and Dexter-Huron launch coordinates. AW also provides numeric Delhi Mills bands on USGS 04174500, but AW warns the Ann Arbor gauge is below Barton and Argo dams and can diverge from the upstream Delhi Mills reach. The source explicitly requires visual confirmation and graph interpretation, so the proxy remains too weak for a new V2 route despite strong access evidence.

gauge.siteName: Slate Creek at Highway 713 near Mt. Sterling, KY
gauge.notes: Direct same-creek USGS gauge named by KDFWR for current stream levels. The official USGS legacy current-conditions page showed May 31, 2026 instantaneous discharge and gage-height values during the add run.

thresholdSupport.notes: KDFWR publishes numeric guidance for Howards Mill downstream to the Licking River: Low below 100 cfs, Good 100-200 cfs, High above 200 cfs. The implemented app model uses those values as a two-sided 100-200 cfs band.

endpoints.coordinateSource: KDFWR access-detail records for Slate Creek - Lions Club and Slate Creek - Old Slate Furnace.

candidates.notes: Added to V2 on 2026-06-01 as slate-creek-lions-club-old-slate-furnace. Caveats: use the downstream Lions Club access below the low-head dam, expect shallow rock bars and dragging below 100 cfs, treat water above 200 cfs as high for casual use, watch for wood and fast rain response, and stay with public access/ordinary legal stops rather than private banks. No gallery image was added because no rights-clean representative route photo was found.

candidates.route: Burnt Mill Bridge to Leatherwood Ford
candidates.retryCondition: Already implemented as big-south-fork-burnt-mill-leatherwood. Revisit only if NPS access status changes, USGS 03410210 stops exposing product-ready live discharge/gage-height values, NPS or American Whitewater updates the gorge level guidance, or the Commons Leatherwood Ford image can be fetched locally.
sourcesChecked: NPS Big South Fork river run descriptions reviewed 2026-05-31; lists Burnt Mill Bridge to Leatherwood Ford as an 11-mile Class III-IV fall/winter/spring run with 20 feet per mile average drop.

NPS Big South Fork river levels reviewed 2026-05-31; provides Leatherwood Ford gauge descriptions at 500, 1000, 1800, 2500, 3500, 5000, 10000, 20000, and 40000 cfs, with 1000 cfs described as optimum for open canoes and 3500 cfs described as powerful with difficult rescue.
NPS Big South Fork river gauges reviewed 2026-05-31; confirms Leatherwood Ford gauge context, 15-minute recalculation, seasonal baseflow swings, and fast-rise warnings after rain.
NPS Big South Fork river access points reviewed 2026-05-31; provides access directions for Burnt Mill Bridge and Leatherwood Ford.

USGS 03410210 South FK Cumberland River at Leatherwood Ford official pages reviewed 2026-05-31; legacy current conditions exposed same-day values of 3860 cfs and 9.80 ft at 2026-05-31 14:30 EDT, and WDFN metadata confirms coordinates 36.4773905730466 / -84.66935079540444.

American Whitewater Big South Fork Confluence-to-Leatherwood page reviewed 2026-05-31; identifies the downstream gorge as Class II-IV wilderness whitewater, gives the Leatherwood gauge relationship, and documents undercuts, sieves, major rapids, limited access, O&W/Leatherwood logistics, and current runnable-flow context.

American Whitewater Clear Fork Burnt Mill-to-Confluence page reviewed 2026-05-31; confirms Burnt Mill Bridge as the upstream Clear Fork put-in, Class II+(III) character before the Big South Fork confluence, and parking at the west end of the bridge.

Rights-clean image pass reviewed Wikimedia Commons. A CC BY 3.0 Commons photo exists from Leatherwood Ford, but the sandbox could not fetch the binary from upload.wikimedia.org, so no gallery asset was added.
aliases: Clear Fork / Big South Fork Gorge - Burnt Mill to Leatherwood
Big South Fork gorge run
Burnt Mill Bridge to Leatherwood Ford Bridge

gauge.notes: USGS 03410210 South FK Cumberland River at Leatherwood Ford is the direct take-out/corridor gauge used by NPS and American Whitewater for Big South Fork gorge level decisions. Official legacy current conditions showed same-day May 31, 2026 discharge and gage-height observations.

thresholdSupport.notes: NPS hosts numeric Leatherwood Ford level descriptions originally provided by whitewater clubs. The implemented guarded model uses tooLow 500 cfs, idealMin 1000 cfs, idealMax 2500 cfs, and tooHigh 3500 cfs. This is strong enough for protected whitewater scoring but is intentionally marked mixed rather than official because it is descriptive gorge guidance, not a modern manager go/no-go table.

endpoints.putIn: Burnt Mill Bridge access on Clear Fork
endpoints.takeOut: Leatherwood Ford access / USGS gauge corridor
endpoints.coordinateSource: Burnt Mill Bridge coordinates are USGS-topo-derived from YellowMaps/USGS GNIS-style bridge data; Leatherwood Ford coordinates are USGS 03410210 WDFN metadata for the take-out/gauge corridor, corroborated by NPS access directions.

candidates.notes: Added to V2 on 2026-05-31 as big-south-fork-burnt-mill-leatherwood. This is the first Tennessee route and is deliberately marked routeType whitewater. Caveats: Class III-IV gorge water, undercuts, sieves, hydraulics, pin rocks, strainers, cold swims, limited exits, steep walk-outs, fast rain rises, remote rescue, park access status, and today-review water above the shipped 3500 cfs high cutoff. No gallery image was added because the qualifying Commons Leatherwood Ford image could not be downloaded in the sandbox.

thresholdSupport.notes: KDFWR publishes official Stearns-gauge bands: below 100 cfs expect dragging, 100-500 cfs best casual floating, 500-1500 cfs faster/open-boat interest, above 1500 cfs experienced closed boats only, and over 3000 cfs extremely dangerous. The implemented route uses that direct official threshold model.

thresholdSupport.notes: OzarkAnglers gives Richwoods-gauge guidance for this section: under 100 cfs is low but floatable with scraping; 100-200 cfs and 200-400 cfs are easily floatable; 400-800 cfs is high but floatable; 800-1200 cfs is very high and dangerous for inexperienced paddlers; over 1200 cfs is too high. V2 uses a conservative two-sided model of 100-800 cfs, with 100 cfs tooLow and 1,200 cfs tooHigh.

candidates.blocker: 2026-06-12 12:58 America/Chicago / 2026-06-12 17:58 UTC blocker refresh. City of Kearney and NGPC/Nebraskaland sources still strongly support the 2.3-mile Yanney Park-to-Central Avenue Kearney Water Trail and local 180-250 cfs flow guidance. No route was added because the direct official live gauge remains Nebraska DWEE 00145700, while PaddleTodayV2 GaugeProvider still supports only usgs and mn_dnr. USGS 06770200 Platte near Kearney returned 1590 cfs and 3.59 ft at 2026-06-12 12:15 CDT, but remains an unacceptable mainstem proxy for the canal/Turkey Creek route.
candidates.retryCondition: Retry only if PaddleTodayV2 adds Nebraska DWEE as a supported live gauge provider or a direct product-supported USGS gauge for the Kearney Water Trail is identified, and public endpoint coordinates plus hazard copy are captured.
sourcesChecked: City of Kearney Water Trail Map reviewed 2026-06-11; confirms Yanney Park to Central Avenue, total distance 2.3 miles, public entrance landing, exit landing, portage/whitewater-feature context, and designed kayaking/canoeing use.
City of Kearney Water Trail etiquette/regulations page reviewed 2026-06-11; confirms boating regulations, PFD requirements, helmet/footwear recommendations, and leave-no-trace rules.

NGPC/Nebraskaland Kearney Water Trail article reviewed 2026-06-11; confirms the 2.3-mile trail, Yanney Park start, Kearney Canal/Turkey Creek route, April-October NPPD hydroelectric operation context, and public/community route background.

NGPC/Nebraskaland Kearney whitewater article reviewed 2026-06-11; says flows at the features vary with natural Turkey Creek flow and NPPD operation, commonly about 150-250 cfs on weekdays when operating, with 180-250 cfs described as best for surfing and higher/lower caveats.

Nebraska DWEE RealTime StationsList reviewed 2026-06-11; Turkey Creek near Kearney station 00145700 showed 169.54 cfs and 1.91 ft at 2026-06-11 16:00 CDT, but DWEE is not a PaddleTodayV2-supported gauge provider.

The PFBC lower Juniata guide publishes exact public access coordinates, a 10-mile route shape, a mile-10.5 ledge warning, and a direct Newport minimum gauge level of 3.5 ft; USGS Water Services returned same-day 2026-06-23 values for direct gauge 01567000.
candidates.retryCondition: Already implemented. Revisit only if PFBC changes the Newport or Amity Hall access package, PFBC updates the Newport minimum guidance, USGS 01567000 stops exposing product-ready current stage, or a better exact-route photo appears.
sourcesChecked: PFBC lower Juniata River Water Trail guide and access table
USGS Water Services IV for 01567000 on 2026-06-23
USGS monitoring location page for Juniata River at Newport, PA
PFBC launch permit vs boat registration page
Wikimedia Commons Juniata River hills in Howe Township file page
aliases: Juniata River - Newport to Amity Hall
Lower Juniata - Newport to Amity Hall PFBC

gauge.notes: USGS Water Services returned same-day 2026-06-23 values of 1,270 cfs and 3.62 ft for Juniata River at Newport, PA.

thresholdSupport.notes: The PFBC lower Juniata guide lists Newport among the useful gauges for the lower Juniata and recommends a minimum level of 3.5 ft there.

endpoints.coordinateSource: The PFBC lower Juniata access table publishes both coordinates directly.

notes: Implemented on 2026-06-23 as a conservative minimum-only lower Juniata route.
Camping is on-route-campsite because Green Valley Campground sits on the route and is the clearest legal overnight support if the run is split.
Hazards carried into implementation: rapid current over the mile-10.5 ledge, private banks, shallow summer bars, broad-river wind, and same-day wood.
No rights-clean exact-route image was approved during this add pass.

candidates.route: Rapidan Dam Park to County Road 90
candidates.blocker: Rejected during route safety audit. Current MN DNR Blue Earth River map notes the Rapidan Dam failed in 2024 and says paddling through the dam area is not recommended because of debris, unstable river channel, and recirculating currents below the dam area. The shipped route began at the Rapidan Dam Park corridor, so warning copy is not enough.
candidates.retryCondition: Reconsider only if MN DNR and Blue Earth County publish updated post-failure route guidance confirming a safe public launch corridor, stable channel conditions, and a recommended route shape that avoids the Rapidan dam hazard.
sourcesChecked: MN DNR Blue Earth River water trail map updated after the 2024 Rapidan Dam failure; warns against paddling through the dam area because of debris, unstable channel, and recirculating currents.
Blue Earth County Rapidan Dam status page reviewed for current dam damage/usability context.
aliases: Blue Earth River - Rapidan Dam Park to County Road 90

gauge.notes: Direct gauge support existed, but safety/access status now blocks route inclusion.

thresholdSupport.notes: MN DNR level guidance exists but does not resolve the failed Rapidan Dam corridor hazard.

candidates.notes: 2026-06-17 safety audit: removed from shipped route data and rejected because the Rapidan Dam failed in 2024 and current official DNR map guidance says not to paddle through the dam area.
